commonIssues: Root rot from overwatering or wet/heavy clay soils is the most common killer; ensure sharp drainage and let soil dry between waterings.
Leaf spot fungal disease in humid, crowded conditions; provide good air circulation.
Declines or is short-lived in hot, humid summers (e.g. USDA zone 8 and warmer humid regions).
In containers the root zone is less cold-hardy than in the ground; in zone 5/6 move pots to a sheltered spot or insulate over winter.
Container minimum of ~8 L reflects a young/dwarf plant; a mature English lavender is better in a 10-18 L (30-40 cm diameter) pot for vigor and winter root insulation.
Wait to plant until spring frost danger passes and soil reaches about 15 C (60 F).

source.citation: The Old Farmer's Almanac - Lavender: How to Plant, Grow, and Care for Lavender Flowers. States full sun at least 6 to 8 hours/day; hardy in Zones 5 and warmer; plant in spring after soil warms to at least 60 F (15 C) and frost danger passes; must drain well, overwatering rots roots, thrives in semiarid conditions.

The Old Farmer's Almanac - Lavender. Hardy in Zones 5 and warmer, overwinters in ground without protection; wait until top inch of soil is dry before watering; modest water needs after establishment; spring planting after soil >= 60 F (15 C).
The Old Farmer's Almanac - Lavender. Spring planting (after soil warms to ~60 F / 15 C and frost passes); hardy Zone 5+.

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Lavandula angustifolia 'Munstead'. USDA zone 5 to 8; full sun; water needs 'dry to medium'; average, dry to medium, well-drained, alkaline soil, light sandy soil with low fertility; height/spread 1.0-1.5 ft; bloom June-August; susceptible to leaf spot and root rot; may not survive winter if soils are not well-drained or temperatures dip below zero degrees F (-17.8 C) without snow cover.
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder - Lavandula angustifolia 'Munstead'. Water needs 'dry to medium'; susceptible to root rot in poorly drained conditions especially in winter.

RHS Growing Guide - How to grow lavender. English types usually hardy and left outside over winter in free-draining soil; in containers water regularly in summer (about once or twice a week in hot weather), keep fairly dry in winter; in the ground rarely needs watering after establishment; containers 30-40 cm diameter; peat-free loam-based compost such as John Innes No.2 plus up to 25% coarse grit; plants don't generally need feeding; plant in April or May, never in winter; full sun.
RHS Growing Guide - How to grow lavender. Plants don't generally need feeding; container plants benefit from minimal/occasional feeding only.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Lavandula angustifolia. USDA hardiness 5a-9b; full sun (6+ hours direct sun) or partial; low water needs, drought tolerant, does not like wet feet and dies in heavy clays; neutral to alkaline pH 6.0-8.0+; evergreen perennial shrub, broadly mounded, 1-2 ft tall x 2-3 ft wide; low maintenance; problems include root rot from overwatering and leaf spot fungal disease (good air circulation helps).
NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Lavandula angustifolia. Evergreen perennial shrub, broadly mounded woody habit; low water; root rot and leaf spot are the common problems.

Confidence note: coldHardyMinC set to -23 C as a conservative value. Sources agree on USDA zone 5 hardiness (zone 5a low ~ -28.9 C; zone 5b low ~ -26.1 C), but Missouri Botanical Garden warns plants may not survive below 0 F (-17.8 C) without snow cover and that container roots are less hardy than ground roots. -23 C is a middle, conservative ground-hardiness figure rather than the absolute zone-5a extreme.

Confidence note: heatStressC set to 38 C. Extension sources (Utah State, others) indicate English lavender performs best below ~105 F (40.6 C) and declines in hot, humid zone 8 summers; 38 C is a conservative onset-of-heat-stress estimate, as no single extension page gave an exact heat-stress threshold in Celsius.

Confidence note: wateringProfile baseIntervalDays of 7 reflects RHS container guidance (water about once or twice weekly in hot summer weather, much less otherwise). In-ground established plants need far less water; 7 days is a conservative container-oriented default for an app, with high dryingTolerance and high overwaterRisk encoding lavender's drought preference and root-rot risk.
